notable.notable_blurb: Son of Yale's founding trustee; Yale tutor 1722-24

narrative_blocks.header: Yale tutor 1722-24 — then apothecary
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated from Yale in 1718 and served as a tutor there 1722-24. He then established himself as an apothecary in Boston, continuing the business until his second marriage in 1754, when he moved to New Haven and carried on the same trade there until his death in 1776.

narrative_blocks.header: His three eldest sons all married sisters
narrative_blocks.body: The eldest three sons — Evelyn, Robert, and James — all married sisters, daughters of Charles Collins of Litchfield. The Collins sisters were daughters of Charles Collins of Litchfield.

narrative_blocks.header: Whitefield preached under his elm tree — twice
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. George Whitefield was twice the guest of James Pierpont — in October 1740 and again in 1745. On the latter occasion "he preached to an immense audience from a platform under the Elm tree, in front of Mr. Pierpont's house." (Pierpont occupied his father's residence at Elm and Temple Streets, New

narrative_blocks.header: Spent his last years chasing an English inheritance
narrative_blocks.body: The last years of his life were clouded by vain efforts to obtain recognition as the rightful heir to the titles and estates of the Pierrepont family in England. (His son Evelyn carried this further — His son Evelyn pursued this claim further.

narrative_blocks.header: Sister married Edwards — the Aaron Burr legacy
narrative_blocks.body: His sister Sarah Pierpont Edwards married Jonathan Edwards, the Great Awakening theologian. Their daughter Esther married Rev. Aaron Burr Sr., and their son Aaron Burr became the 3rd Vice President of the United States and killed Alexander Hamilton in a duel in 1804. The Pierpont siblings split into two of the most consequential American lineages.

bio.bio_blurb: Yale tutor, then apothecary in Boston and New Haven

career.notes: Occupied his father’s residence at Elm and Temple Streets.

notable.notable_blurb: Son of Yale's founding trustee; Yale tutor 1722-24

narrative_blocks.body: He graduated from Yale in 1718 and served as a tutor there 1722-24. He then established himself as an apothecary in Boston, continuing the business until his second marriage in 1754, when he moved to New Haven and carried on the same trade there until his death in 1776.

On the latter occasion "he preached to an immense audience from a platform under the Elm tree, in front of Mr. Pierpont's house." (Pierpont occupied his father's residence at Elm and Temple Streets, New

narrative_blocks.body: The last years of his life were clouded by vain efforts to obtain recognition as the rightful heir to the titles and estates of the Pierrepont family in England. (His son Evelyn carried this further — His son Evelyn pursued this claim further.

narrative_blocks.body: His sister Sarah Pierpont Edwards married Jonathan Edwards, the Great Awakening theologian. Their daughter Esther married Rev. Aaron Burr Sr., and their son Aaron Burr became the 3rd Vice President of the United States and killed Alexander Hamilton in a duel in 1804. The Pierpont siblings split into two of the most consequential American lineages.

bio.bio_blurb: Yale tutor, then apothecary in Boston and New Haven

career.notes: Occupied his father’s residence at Elm and Temple Streets.

bio.notes: Also given as Ann Sherman Davenport — widow of a Davenport at time of marriage

burial.plot_notes: Fair Haven Union Cemetery, New Haven CT. GPS: 41.31140, -72.89440.

cross_connections.display_label: Friend-compatriots in the Revolutionary War.

narrative_blocks.header: Parliament refused to make him Duke because he
narrative_blocks.body: His descent from the Dukes of Kingston was formally established — but Parliament refused to recognize the claim because he was American. After the Revolution he became financially reduced and spent his final years working for wages at Yale, the institution his own family had helped found.

narrative_blocks.header: Three brothers snowbound — three sisters married
narrative_blocks.body: Three Pierpont brothers journeying through Litchfield County stopped overnight at the Collins home in South Farms when a great storm stranded them for weeks. All three married daughters of the family: Robert married Lois Collins, his brother Evelyn married her sister Rhoda, and their brother James married a third sister.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale alumnus turned Yale College employee
narrative_blocks.body: Evelyn was a Revolutionary War officer and a Yale College graduate who settled as a farmer at Litchfield Connecticut after the war. Reduced in circumstances late in life, he returned to Yale — not as alumnus but as employee — and continued in such service until his death.

career.notes: Pastorate after move to Rochester area in 1821; 'several years' per Pierpont family book.
Later pastorate in Avon — site of the family burial ground (LittleVille Cemetery).

narrative_blocks.header: Reverted name spelling to ancestral Pierrepont
narrative_blocks.body: Hezekiah Beers consciously restored the older spelling 'Pierrepont' to the family name, undoing the simplified 'Pierpont' his immediate American forebears had adopted. The change held in his Rochester branch — his son Jonathan Edwards Pierrepont and granddaughter Caroline Editha Pierrepont (later Ward) carry the Reverend's preferred spelling forward.

narrative_blocks.header: Would have inherited the lost English titles
narrative_blocks.body: Per family tradition, when Hezekiah's older brother Evelyn died at sea off Norway in 1812 unmarried, Hezekiah became the head of the American branch — and would have inherited the disputed English Pierrepont titles and estates had the family's claim to them ever been recognized. The Pierrepont book treats this with appropriate skepticism.

death.cause: Died while visiting daughter Laura (Mrs. Col. Anson Sperry) at Plattsburgh.

burial.plot_notes: Dellwood Cemetery, Manchester, Bennington County, Vermont. GPS: 43.15250, -73.07530.

narrative_blocks.header: Three brothers snowbound — three sisters married
narrative_blocks.body: Three Pierpont brothers journeying through Litchfield County stopped overnight at the Collins home in South Farms when a great storm stranded them for weeks. All three married daughters of the family: Robert married Lois Collins, his brother Evelyn married her sister Rhoda, and their brother James married a third sister.

cross_connections.display_label: is the cousin whose son George married his daughter Sarah

narrative_blocks.header: Three brothers snowbound — three sisters married
narrative_blocks.body: Three Pierpont brothers journeying through Litchfield County stopped overnight at the Collins home in South Farms when a great storm stranded them for weeks. All three married daughters of the family: Robert married Lois Collins, his brother Evelyn married her sister Rhoda, and their brother James married a third sister.

sources.short: Find a Grave — Cholera Cemetery Sandusky OH

death.cause: Drowned in Lake Erie when sailboat capsized in a squall off Put-in-Bay (South Bass Island)

cross_connections.display_label: War of 1812 soldier died at Sandusky, 1813
cross_connections.notes: Both Hooker descendants died at Sandusky, Ohio, twenty-three years apart. Norman Hart (gen 7, Marietta Hart line) died there August 1813 as a soldier of the War of 1812 aged seventeen — likely at the August 1-2 defense of Fort Stephenson. Sherman drowned in Lake Erie there May 7, 1836 aged fifty-two. Different lines of Hooker descent ending at the same Ohio frontier town a generation apart.

cross_connections.display_label: Drowned at Lake Garfield Massachusetts, 1915, age 38
cross_connections.notes: Both Hooker descendants drowned in lakes. Albert Norton Butler — Yale 1900, president of Butler & Klein Lumber Co. — drowned in Lake Garfield at Monterey, Berkshire County Massachusetts on September 28, 1915 when his rowboat capsized; aged thirty-eight. Seventy-nine years and two generations after Sherman's Lake Erie drowning at Sandusky in 1836.

career.title: Wool merchant
career.notes: Continued the Pierpont family wool business in Litchfield County. When Sherman and his wife Sidney moved to Sandusky Ohio (probably early 1830s), his younger brother James Morris Pierpont IV (H00832) took over the business. Per My Country (vol. 28 no. 1, winter 1994).

burial.plot_notes: Buried May 27, 1836 after body washed ashore on Lake Erie. His name appears on a Find A Grave memorial with parents and siblings at Morris Burying Ground in Litchfield County CT, but this is in error — actual burial is at Cholera Cemetery in Sandusky OH per FG records.

narrative_blocks.header: Drowned in Lake Erie squall, body washed ashore
narrative_blocks.body: Sherman left Put-in-Bay on a sailboat heading for Sandusky Ohio May 7, 1836 with two other men, and all three drowned when the boat capsized in a squall. His body washed ashore on Lake Erie twenty days later. His brother Rev. John Pierpont, the Boston abolitionist, wrote in a poem of dreaming of his lost brother 'sleeping on Low Sandusky's shore.'

narrative_blocks.header: Body washed ashore three weeks after drowning
narrative_blocks.body: His sailboat capsized in a squall off Put-in-Bay on South Bass Island in Lake Erie on 7 May 1836; all three men aboard drowned. His body was discovered 27 May 1836 on the shore of Lake Erie — three weeks after the squall — and brought to Sandusky for burial.

narrative_blocks.header: Brother John's poem mourned him from Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: His older brother Rev John Pierpont wrote a posthumous poem preserved in the Rhoda Cowles Diary: 'I am awakened by my weeping / To know that thou art sleeping / on Low Sandusky's shore.' The grief crossed both the family wool business and the geographic separation of Connecticut from Ohio.

cross_connections.display_label: Cousins, both prominent: Boston Unitarian minister and Vermont Chief Justice.

institutions.institution_blurb: Studied law there before turning to Unitarian ministry.

narrative_blocks.header: Boston Unitarian who fought slavery from the pulpit
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Pastor of Boston's Hollis-Street Church from 1819 to 1845, one of America's most prominent Unitarian pulpits. From there he published anti-slavery poems, championed temperance, and earned a national reputation as a reformer.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1804 lawyer turned poet and minister
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale College in 1804 and trained at Litchfield Law School before practicing in Newburyport, Massachusetts. He left law for the Unitarian ministry, publishing his volume of religious verse 'Airs of Palestine' to wide acclaim.

narrative_blocks.header: Grandfather of J.P. Morgan
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Juliet Pierpont married Junius Spencer Morgan in 1836, making him the maternal grandfather of financier J. Pierpont Morgan. The Morgan banking dynasty descends from him through this single marriage.

narrative_blocks.header: His son James composed 'Jingle Bells' in 1857
narrative_blocks.body: His son James Lord Pierpont (1822-1893) composed 'Jingle Bells' in 1857, originally as a Thanksgiving song. The song became one of the most-recorded Christmas standards of the 20th century.

narrative_blocks.header: Served as Civil War chaplain at seventy-six
narrative_blocks.body: He volunteered as a regimental chaplain in 1861 at age 76 — among the oldest serving in the Union Army. The age was no obstacle to his commitment to the abolitionist cause.

narrative_blocks.header: Sally died young
narrative_blocks.body: Sally died in 1794, aged about eight.

sources.short: Hyde Genealogy by Reuben H. Walworth, p.300-301

institutions.institution_blurb: Studied at Sarah Pierce's school in 1807 — ledger record at Litchfield Historical Society.

education.institution: Sarah Pierce's Female Academy (Litchfield Female Academy)
education.notes: Studied at Sarah Pierce's Female Academy (Litchfield CT) in 1807 — same school her mother Elizabeth Collins Pierpont and her brother John Pierpont attended. Per Litchfield Historical Society Ledger.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband married her sister while she still lived
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. John Langdon — Yale 1809 minister whom Elizabeth Pierpont had married on March 5, 1817 in Bethlehem Connecticut — also married her younger sister Abby Pierpont Church on December 9, 1824, while Elizabeth was still alive. Elizabeth died nine months later on September 21, 1825 at age thirty-three. Rev. Langdon himself died February 28, 1830, having been married to both Pierpont sisters; the second marriage during the first's lifetime was extraordinary even for the relatively elastic Litchfield Hill Congregational marriage norms of the period.

narrative_blocks.header: Studied at Sarah Pierce's Litchfield Female Academy, 1807
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Pierpont's name appears in the 1807 ledger of Sarah Pierce's Female Academy in Litchfield Connecticut — the celebrated school for daughters of the New England elite that operated 1792-1833 and educated several thousand women including Catharine Beecher and Harriet Beecher Stowe. She would have been fifteen or seventeen years old (depending on birth-year source) when she studied there. The ledger record survives at the Litchfield Historical Society.

narrative_blocks.header: Birth date recorded 1790 or 1792 across sources
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Pierpont's birth date is given variously as May 28, 1790 or May 28, 1792 across surviving records — a two-year uncertainty that has not been resolved by primary documentation. Her death is recorded similarly variously as 1823 or 1825. The unresolved dates were typical of early-Republic Litchfield-Hill Connecticut documentation, where domestic record-keeping passed unevenly between family Bibles, town records, and church baptismal ledgers.

narrative_blocks.header: James died young
narrative_blocks.body: James died in 1793, aged about four. James’s parents gave the name to a child born seven years later.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sons lost to one civil war
narrative_blocks.body: Leonard Pierpont, who had moved his family from Litchfield, Connecticut, to the Illinois prairie, sent his sons to the Civil War and lost three of them. Leonard Jr. died of wounds at Galveston, Walter died a prisoner of war in Virginia, and Edward Sherman was killed at Fort Blakeley in the western theater's last battle — all within about a year.

burial.plot_notes: East Cemetery, Litchfield CT. Buried beside wife Sarah.

narrative_blocks.header: The one Pierpont brother who chose a different
narrative_blocks.body: His three brothers Evelyn, Robert, and James were snowbound at the Collins farmhouse and each married a Collins daughter; David alone married Sarah Phelps of Litchfield, settling near his brothers but taking a different path. All four brothers spent their lives within a few miles of each other in Litchfield County.

cross_connections.display_label: Robert Pierpoint began studying law at age 16 with Governor.
Robert Pierpoint began studying law at age 16 with Governor.
Robert Pierpoint began studying law at age 16 with Governor.
Robert Pierpoint (Lt. Governor of Vermont 1848-1850) and his brother.

narrative_blocks.header: Sixteenth Lieutenant Governor of Vermont, 1848-1850
narrative_blocks.body: He served as the sixteenth Lieutenant Governor of Vermont 1848-1850 (Whig). Previously: VT House 1819 and 1823, Governor's Council 1825-1831, two delegate appointments to VT constitutional conventions, VT Senate 1836-1840.

narrative_blocks.header: Vermont Circuit Court Judge, 1850-1856
narrative_blocks.body: After his Lt. Governor term ended he served as a Vermont Circuit Court judge from 1850 to 1856 — moving from executive to judicial office in the same state where his brother John D. Pierpont served as chief justice.

narrative_blocks.header: Studied law at sixteen with the Vermont Governor
narrative_blocks.body: At sixteen he began studying law in Rutland VT with Richard Skinner — future Governor of Vermont. Skinner had married Frances Pierpont, the young Robert's own cousin; he was effectively studying law in the house of his cousin's husband.

narrative_blocks.header: War of 1812 tax collector at twenty-one
narrative_blocks.body: In his early twenties during the War of 1812 he served as U.S. Tax Collector for Vermont — a politically sensitive Federal position during the war years. He went on to serve as Rutland County Clerk from 1820 to 1839.

cross_connections.display_label: Cousins, both prominent: Vermont Chief Justice and Boston Unitarian minister.

institutions.institution_blurb: Attended 1825-1826; later Chief Justice of Vermont Supreme Court.

narrative_blocks.header: Chief Justice of Vermont Supreme Court
narrative_blocks.body: He served on the Vermont Supreme Court from 1857, was elevated to Chief Justice in 1865, and held the position until his death in 1882. The court expanded from three to six justices during his tenure.

narrative_blocks.header: Switched parties three times over slavery
narrative_blocks.body: He started as a Democrat, joined the Free Soil Party in opposition to slavery, then became a Republican. The path took him from local probate clerk to chairman of the Vermont Senate Judiciary Committee.

narrative_blocks.header: Twenty-one years as Addison County probate clerk
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Register of Probate for Addison County from 1836 to 1857. The role oversaw inheritance and estate matters across the county for two decades before his judicial career.

narrative_blocks.header: His Boston cousin's grandson founded J.P. Morgan
narrative_blocks.body: He and the abolitionist poet Rev. John Pierpont were first cousins through Hooker descent. The poet's grandson J. Pierpont Morgan founded the New York financial house.

education.notes: — Litchfield Law School 1825-1826. Chief Justice Vermont Supreme Court 1865-1882.

cross_connections.display_label: East Guilford ordination, November 25, 1706
East Guilford ordination, November 25, 1706
East Guilford ordination, November 25, 1706
Both founding trustees of Yale 1701; Pierson was first rector.
Pierpont built the institution Davenport had envisioned.
Descendant co-founded Skull and Bones at his Yale 130 years later.
coined the Ivy League name for colleges like Yale
his brother-in-law, a fellow Yale founder married to a Hooker sister

institutions.institution_blurb: Attended Roxbury Latin School before Harvard.

artworks.artwork_blurb: Portrait 1711 by Pierpont Limner; Yale University Art Gallery.

narrative_blocks.header: Ten ministers, forty books, one college
narrative_blocks.body: In 1700 Pierpont gathered ten ministers at Branford, each laying down volumes with the words “I give these books for the founding of a College in this Colony” — tradition credits James with six of the original forty. The charter he secured the next year became Yale.

narrative_blocks.header: Two elms planted at the minister’s door
narrative_blocks.body: A poor farmer with nothing else to give carried two young elms on his shoulder and planted them before the new parsonage in 1686. Under their shade Jonathan Edwards courted Sarah Pierpont forty years on, and Whitefield preached at sixty. When the last elm finally came down, its sound trunk was sawed into blocks and scattered among the family.

narrative_blocks.header: Three brides in seven years
narrative_blocks.body: James buried two young wives in five years — Abigail, dead of a cold caught attending church in her wedding garments per the custom of the day, and Sarah, lost after childbirth. In 1698 he married Mary Hooker of Farmington, who bore eight children and outlived him by twenty-six years.

narrative_blocks.header: He drafted Connecticut’s church constitution
narrative_blocks.body: A leader of the 1708 Saybrook Synod, Pierpont is said to have drawn up the Articles of the Saybrook Platform — the system of discipline and fellowship that governed Connecticut’s churches for more than a century.

narrative_blocks.header: 1706 ordination — five ministers, all Hooker-tree
narrative_blocks.body: On November 25, 1706 he was among five ministers who ordained Rev. John Hart at East Guilford — alongside Buckingham of Saybrook, Noadiah Russell of Middletown, Samuel Russell of Branford, and Thomas Ruggles of Guilford. All five names run through the Hooker tree.

narrative_blocks.header: The household that seeded three centuries
narrative_blocks.body: The New Haven parsonage became one of America’s great seedbeds: daughter Sarah married Jonathan Edwards, and the lines that followed carried two Timothy Dwights and Theodore Dwight Woolsey to Yale’s presidency, a vice president named Burr — and, further on, a financier named John Pierpont Morgan.

education.notes: Oldest boys’ school in the colonies, founded 1645.

career.notes: Ordained July 2 1685, ending an eleven-year pulpit vacancy; served until death.
The school became Yale College in 1718.

cross_connections.display_label: fathered the New Haven minister she married in 1698
was mother to the New Haven minister she married in 1698
her granddaughter, last of the line the heirloom apron passed through

notable.notable_blurb: Wife of Yale co-founder Rev. James Pierpont

artworks.artwork_blurb: Companion portrait to husband Rev. James Pierpont, 1711; Yale Art Gallery.

narrative_blocks.header: Eight children in the house under the elms
narrative_blocks.body: Mary ran the New Haven parsonage through sixteen years of marriage and eight children, then presided over it as a widow for twenty-six more. The congregation had raised the house by voluntary subscription, and it stood for over a century.

narrative_blocks.header: Married James Pierpont the year her father died
narrative_blocks.body: Her father, the Farmington minister Samuel Hooker, died in November 1697; she married James Pierpont of New Haven the following year. The match carried her from one parsonage to the center of Connecticut’s emerging intellectual capital.

narrative_blocks.header: Her daughter’s courtship under the elms
narrative_blocks.body: It was Mary’s daughter Sarah whom Jonathan Edwards courted beneath the parsonage elms in 1726 — a match made in her widowed household that joined the Pierpont and Edwards lines and led on to college presidents and a vice president.

narrative_blocks.header: A son lost crossing the Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: Her son Samuel — Yale 1718, newly ordained at Lyme — drowned in March 1723 crossing the Connecticut River in a canoe with an Indian guide. His body came ashore on Fishers Island six weeks later and was buried where it was found.

narrative_blocks.header: The family that named an artist
narrative_blocks.body: The unidentified colonial painter who took her portrait around 1711 is known to art history only as the Pierpont Limner — christened for this family, whose likenesses anchor his surviving work. Her husband’s companion portrait hangs at the Yale University Art Gallery.

narrative_blocks.header: A silk apron may trace to her line
narrative_blocks.body: A rare 1730s embroidered silk apron at the Connecticut Historical Society descends through three generations of Marys beginning with her. Though scholars doubt she stitched it herself, her advantageous marriage made such a luxury possible for the daughters who followed.

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Mrs. James Pierpont (née Mary Hooker), 1711. Wikipedia Commons.

career.notes: Also selectman, town officer, and magistrate.

narrative_blocks.header: He fortified his mills in Philip’s War
narrative_blocks.body: During King Philip’s War in 1675-6, John fortified his Roxbury homestead, “where were malting and mills for grinding and fulling, God having blessed him with a considerable estate.” The tide-mill’s foundation timbers were still visible two centuries later.

narrative_blocks.header: A son who carried Roxbury to Carolina
narrative_blocks.body: His son Benjamin led a company from Roxbury in 1691 to found an independent church in South Carolina, and died in the ministry at Charleston — while James, the son who stayed, founded a college in Connecticut.

cross_connections.display_label: married his son James, the minister who founded Yale

narrative_blocks.header: She died when James was five
narrative_blocks.body: Thankful died at thirty-five, when her son James — the future founder of Yale — was five years old. Her father, Deacon John Stow, had given the land that seated the Pierponts in Roxbury.

cross_connections.display_label: married her son James, the minister who founded Yale

burial.plot_notes: No stone survives. Well-attested tradition places the grave at the south side of Rev. Samuel Whitman's marked grave. "Mr. Whitman like Mr. Hooker, died while the minister of the town, and tradition says he was buried by the side of Mr. Hooker."

sources.notes: John Winthrop Jr., Alchemy and the Creation of New England Culture, 1606-1676 (Homer Babbidge Prize)

narrative_blocks.header: He inherited his father's library — and his calling
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker's will gave his library to John on condition that John pursue ministry; if John faltered, the entire library and manuscripts went to Samuel. John did not become a minister. Samuel received his father's books — and then dedicated his own life to the ministry his father had hoped for.

narrative_blocks.header: Prosecutor in the Hartford witch trials
narrative_blocks.body: In the 1660s he formed a prosecutorial tribunal with Rev. Samuel Stone of Hartford and Rev. Joseph Haynes of Wethersfield. Connecticut tried witches more aggressively than Massachusetts in this decade — three decades before the Salem trials.

narrative_blocks.header: Ordered to King Philip's War, then quietly replaced
narrative_blocks.body: On March 9, 1675-6, the Council ordered Hooker to prepare to march into King Philip's War with Major Treat's army toward Hadley and Northampton. Two days later, the Council quietly replaced him with Rev. Israel Chauncy. No reason was recorded — only the substitution.

narrative_blocks.header: He persuaded Farmington to found a free school
narrative_blocks.body: In December 1685, the town of Farmington voted to give £30 for a schoolmaster for one year — and declared it a free school for the town, almost certainly at Hooker's instigation. It was among the earliest free public schools in colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: The 'Fervent Hooker,' who preached from the heart
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Pitkin described him as 'an excellent preacher, his composition good, his address pathetic, warm and engaging.' Hooker told a friend he had three things to do with his sermons before delivering them: write them, commit them to memory, and get them into his heart.

cross_connections.display_label: Five Hooker generations on the same Farmington ground.
Farmington's first pastor, whom Hooker succeeded
His grandfather Tobie Willet sailed with John Talcott on the Lion.
Her husband's Prudden line connects to his wife Mary Willet's family
was his father-in-law; First Mayor of New York City, 1665

quotes.text: Thus we have to this day among us our dead Hooker, yet living in his worthy son Samuel Hooker, an able, faithful, useful minister at Farmington, in the Colony of Connecticut.

education.notes: B.A. 1653. Entered Harvard 1651. Chosen Fellow of the College November 27, 1654.

burial.plot_notes: Near center of burying ground, near East Norwalk railway station

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of New York City's first mayor
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Capt. Thomas Willet was appointed the first Mayor of New York City in 1665, when the English renamed the Dutch colony. He had previously succeeded Capt. Miles Standish as commander of Plymouth's militia — one of the most storied military commands in early New England. Mary carried this distinguished lineage to Farmington when she married Rev. Samuel Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Three generations of Willet women known for beauty
narrative_blocks.body: Mary's mother was described as possessing 'beauty and majesty almost divine.' A portrait of Mary's daughter Mary Hooker Pierpont survives, and from it contemporaries formed 'the opinion that she was a woman of uncommon beauty.' Her daughter Sarah was 'accounted the most accomplished lady that had ever come to Norwalk.'

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried at 66 — outlived two minister husbands
narrative_blocks.body: After Samuel Hooker's death in 1697, Mary married Reverend Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook at age 66. When he died in 1709 she moved to Norwalk to live with her son-in-law Reverend Stephen Buckingham, where she remained until her death in 1712 at age 74.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her daughter married father and son
narrative_blocks.body: After Rev. Samuel Hooker died, Mary Willet married Rev. Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook in 1703. Her daughter Sarah was the wife of his son, Rev. Stephen Buckingham, so a mother and daughter had married a father and his son.

cross_connections.display_label: Her grandfather Tobie Willet sailed to New England with John Talcott.
Her father Thomas Willet's widow's first husband was ancestor of H00153's husband

burialCemetery.name: Center Church on the Green Churchyard
alt_names: First Church of Christ Burial Ground
burialCemetery.notes: Historic underground crypt beneath Center Church on the New Haven Green, with associated outdoor burial ground. Burial place of Martha Newton Whittlesey (H00143) and Elizabeth Susanna Whittlesey Barnes (HD0101).

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
